perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, in connection with the business relationship between the parties, PHCS will disclose confidential information to the Recipient; and WHEREAS, the Recipient has been advised of and acknowledges the competitive value and proprietary nature of the confidential information of PHCS and the damage that could result to PHCS if its confidential information is not treated in accordance with the terms and conditions of set forth in this Agreement; NOW THEREF ORE, in consideration the mutual premises and undertakings contained herein, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which is hereby acknowledged, the parties hereto, intending to be legally bound hereby, agree as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, in connection with the business relationship between the parties, PHCS will disclose confidential information to the Recipient; NOW THEREF ORE, in consideration the mutual premises and undertakings contained herein, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which is hereby acknowledged, the parties hereto, intending to be legally bound hereby, agree as follows:
perturbation.explanation: Removing the Recipient's acknowledgment of the confidential information's value and potential damage weakens PHCS's legal standing to claim damages if the information is mishandled. This contradicts implied covenants of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of the acknowledgement weakens the contract's assertion that the recipient understands the value of the information, which is critical for later enforcing a breach. New Jersey law imposes an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. This requires that neither party do anything which will have the effect of destroying or injuring the right of the other party to receive the fruits of the contract. The Recipient's awareness of the value of the information is directly relevant to the fairness of any future dispute.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the Recipient's acknowledgment significantly weakens the contract's foundation for enforcing confidentiality, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith. The altered text removes a key element demonstrating the Recipient's understanding of the confidential information's value and potential harm from misuse. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing N.J. Rev. Stat. § 12A:1-304, establishes an 'obligation of good faith' in every contract. Removing the acknowledgment undermines this obligation because it makes it more difficult to prove the Recipient acted in bad faith if they later mishandle the confidential information, as their initial understanding and agreement were explicitly acknowledged in the original text, but not in the modified text.

perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” means all oral and written information furnished by, or on behalf of, PHCS to the Recipient or its Representatives (as defined below), which is not ge nerally known to the public, including, without limitation, all trade secrets, products, procedures, manuals, guidelines, reports, communic ations, information regarding names and requirements of current and prospective suppliers, vendors and customers, tec hnical information regarding current and prospective products and programs and the development and/or acquisition of future products, programs a nd enhancements, discoveries, concepts and ideas, nature and results of research and development activities, tec hnical information on product or program performance and reliability, processes, formulae, techniques, “know -how,” specifications, any “protected health information” as defined in HIPAA regulations, 45 C.F.R. Parts 160 and 164, as amended from time to time , and any other material or information related to the business or activities of PHCS, whether or not protectible by patent, copyright or other laws, whe ther furnished before or after the date of this Agreement, together with any additional information der ived from such information and contained in all analyses, compilations, studies or other documents or records prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives, as well as al l copies and other reproductions thereof, whether in writing or stored or maintaine d in or by electronic, magnetic or other means, media or devices.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” means all oral and written information furnished by, or on behalf of, PHCS to the Recipient or its Representatives (as defined below), which is not ge nerally known to the public, including, without limitation, all trade secrets, products, procedures, manuals, guidelines, reports, communic ations, information regarding names and requirements of current and prospective suppliers, vendors and customers, tec hnical information regarding current and prospective products and programs and the development and/or acquisition of future products, programs a nd enhancements, discoveries, concepts and ideas, nature and results of research and development activities, tec hnical information on product or program performance and reliability, processes, formulae, techniques, “know -how,” specifications, and any other material or information related to the business or activities of PHCS, whether or not protectible by patent, copyright or other laws, whe ther furnished before or after the date of this Agreement, together with any additional information der ived from such information and contained in all analyses, compilations, studies or other documents or records prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives, as well as al l copies and other reproductions thereof, whether in writing or stored or maintaine d in or by electronic, magnetic or other means, media or devices.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'any “protected health information” as defined in HIPAA regulations, 45 C.F.R. Parts 160 and 164, as amended from time to time' creates ambiguity about whether HIPAA-protected information is covered by the confidentiality agreement. This presents a legal risk, as PHCS needs to guarantee that the recipient understands and agrees to comply with the HIPAA regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA mandates specific protections for health information. By removing the explicit mention of HIPAA, the contract fails to clearly define the responsibilities of the Recipient regarding protected health information, potentially leading to violations of federal law regarding privacy and security of health information if PHCS shares such information and there is a breach.

All covered entities, except "small health plans," must have been compliant with the Privacy Rule by April 14, 2003. 90 Small health plans, however, had until April 14, 2004 to comply. Small Health Plans. A health plan with annual receipts of not more than $5 million is a small health plan. 91 Health plans that file certain federal tax returns and report receipts on those returns should use the guidance provided by the Small Business Administration at 13 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 121.104 to calculate annual receipts. Health plans that do not report receipts to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), for example, group health plans regulated by the Employee Retirement Income Security Act 1974 (ERISA) that are exempt from filing income tax returns, should use proxy measures to determine their annual receipts. 92 See What constitutes a small health plan? perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly includes 'any “protected health information” as defined in HIPAA regulations, 45 C.F.R. Parts 160 and 164, as amended from time to time,' ensuring that such information falls under the confidentiality agreement. The changed text omits this phrase. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight the legal risk and potential violations of HIPAA due to this omission. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 45 CFR 164.502, which broadly governs the uses and disclosures of protected health information. While the snippet itself is incomplete, its citation to this section underscores the importance of adhering to HIPAA regulations regarding health information. By removing the explicit reference to HIPAA, the contract introduces a significant risk of non-compliance, as the recipient might not fully understand or be aware of their responsibilities regarding protected health information under HIPAA.

perturbation.original_text: Upon PHCS’ request, all Confidential Information (and all copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part thereof), whether in writing or stored or maintained in or by electronic, magnetic or other means, media or devices, sh all be returned or destroyed (such destruction to be certified in writing) by an authorized officer and not retained in any form or for any reason.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon PHCS’ request, all Confidential Information (and all copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part thereof), whether in writing or stored or maintained in or by electronic, magnetic or other means, media or devices, sh all be returned or destroyed and not retained in any form or for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'such destruction to be certified in writing by an authorized officer' eliminates a crucial verification step. Without written certification by an authorized officer, there's no verifiable proof the information was actually destroyed as required, which could lead to legal disputes and compliance issues.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Contract Law - Burden of Proof
perturbation.law_explanation: In a breach of contract claim, the burden of proof lies on the party claiming the breach (PHCS). Removing the requirement for a written certification of destruction makes it significantly more difficult for PHCS to prove that the Recipient properly destroyed the confidential information. This weakens PHCS's legal position and contradicts general principles of contract law relating to evidence and burden of proof in New Jersey.
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'such destruction to be certified in writing by an authorized officer' creates a legal contradiction. This removal eliminates a crucial step for verifiable proof of destruction of confidential information, making it difficult for PHCS to prove compliance. While no specific law snippet is provided, the Law Explanation highlights that this weakens PHCS's ability to prove breach of contract. The original text provided a mechanism for verifiable destruction, but the changed text does not.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and co nstrued in accordance with the laws of the State of New Jersey without giving effect to the conflict of laws provisions thereof .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and co nstrued in accordance with the laws of the State of New Jersey.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'without giving effect to the conflict of laws provisions thereof,' the contract becomes ambiguous regarding which state's law will apply in a dispute if there are conflicting laws between New Jersey and another state where the Recipient is located or operates.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles/Conflict of Laws
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the phrase regarding 'conflict of laws' creates uncertainty in the enforcement of the contract. Without that phrase, it is less clear whether the parties intended for *only* New Jersey law to apply regardless of where a dispute is adjudicated. This can create a conflict between the parties if one party is from another state.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The removal of 'without giving effect to the conflict of laws provisions thereof' creates a legal contradiction. The original text clearly indicates that New Jersey law governs without regard to conflict of law principles. The modified text creates ambiguity regarding which state's law applies in a dispute where conflicting laws exist between New Jersey and another jurisdiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions, indicating that the outcome of a case depends on which jurisdiction's law is used. Removing the conflict of laws provision introduces uncertainty and potential for disputes over which law should govern, directly contradicting the intent to apply New Jersey law regardless of conflicts.
